original_question: ( True and not True and False ) is
subquestion: Create a Boolean expression involving four Boolean variables (A, B, C, D) such that the expression evaluates to True if and only if exactly two of the variables are True. Provide the expression and explain why it meets this requirement.
answer:
\[ (A \land B \land 
eg C \land 
eg D) \lor (A \land C \land 
eg B \land 
eg D) \lor (A \land D \land 
eg B \land 
eg C) \lor (B \land C \land 
eg A \land 
eg D) \lor (B \land D \land 
eg A \land 
eg C) \lor (C \land D \land 
eg A \land 
eg B) \lor (A \land B \land C \land 
eg D) \]
judge_answer:
The provided expression does not meet the requirement because it includes terms where more than two variables are true. For instance, the term \( (A \land B \land C \land 
eg D) \) would make the expression true when three of the variables are true, which violates the condition of having exactly two true variables.

A correct Boolean expression for the given requirement would be:

\[ (A \land B \land 
eg C \land 
eg D) \lor (A \land 
eg B \land C \land 
eg D) \lor (A \land 
eg B \land 
eg C \land D) \lor (
eg A \land B \land C \land 
eg D) \lor (
eg A \land B \land 
eg C \land D) \lor (
eg A \land 
eg B \land C \land D) \]

This expression ensures that exactly two out of the four variables are true for the expression to evaluate to true.

FINAL ANSWER: incorrect
